disease.name: Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy (AFLP)
disease.note: Life-threatening obstetric emergency characterized by microvesicular fatty infiltration of hepatocytes in third trimester. Incidence 1:7,000-15,000 pregnancies. Caused by mitochondrial fatty acid oxidation defects (LCHAD deficiency) in the fetus, with maternal hepatotoxicity from accumulated metabolites. High maternal mortality (10-15%) and perinatal mortality (15-25%). Early recognition and prompt delivery are essential.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Suspected AFLP -> EMERGENCY delivery; Fulminant/encephalopathy -> ICU + transplant center; Early/mild -> EXPEDITE delivery after steroids if <34w

presentingComplaints: Nausea and vomiting (often the first symptom)
Right upper abdominal or epigastric pain
Yellowing of the skin and eyes

historyFeatures.note: Often the first symptom; severe and persistent
Due to diabetes insipidus-like state from vasopressinase
Associated with LCHAD deficiency in male fetus

examFeatures.note: Liver may be small/normal in late stage

bedside.name: Blood glucose by glucometer
bedside.indication: hypoglycemia is a hallmark of AFLP; frequent monitoring required

laboratory.name: LFTs (ALT, AST, ALP, GGT, total and direct bilirubin)
laboratory.indication: elevated transaminases (typically 300-1000, moderate elevation), elevated bilirubin (mixed), elevated ALP

laboratory.name: FBC with platelets
laboratory.indication: thrombocytopenia (mild-moderate); less severe than HELLP; leukocytosis common

laboratory.name: INR/PT/PTT
laboratory.indication: prolonged PT/INR due to synthetic dysfunction and DIC

laboratory.name: Serum glucose
laboratory.indication: hypoglycemia is characteristic; from impaired gluconeogenesis and glycogen depletion

laboratory.name: Uric acid
laboratory.indication: markedly elevated early in AFLP; helps distinguish from HELLP

laboratory.name: LDH
laboratory.indication: mildly elevated; less than in HELLP

laboratory.name: Haptoglobin
laboratory.indication: may be normal or slightly decreased

laboratory.name: Swansea Criteria for AFLP diagnosis
laboratory.indication: 6 or more of: vomiting, abdominal pain, polydipsia, bilirubin >14, hypoglycemia <4, urate >340, WCC >11, elevated transaminases, elevated NH3, renal impairment, coagulopathy, microvesicular steatosis, ascites/bright liver on US

imaging.name: Abdominal ultrasound
imaging.indication: bright/hyperechoic liver (fatty infiltration); may appear normal in early disease; ascites; excludes gallstones

imaging.name: CT abdomen (non-contrast)
imaging.indication: liver hypodensity compared to spleen (fatty infiltration); use only if diagnosis uncertain

imaging.name: Liver biopsy
imaging.indication: gold standard: microvesicular steatosis (foamy hepatocytes with central nucleus); rarely performed clinically (risk of bleeding); diagnosis usually clinical using Swansea criteria

management.protocolReference: ACOG Practice Bulletin: Liver Disease in Pregnancy; SHEA Guideline: Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy; Swansea Criteria

pathways.criteria: Third trimester (usually >30w) with nausea/vomiting, RUQ pain, jaundice, + Swansea criteria >=6; or high clinical suspicion
pathways.treatment: URGENT admission to ICU/HDU. Multidisciplinary team: obstetrics, hepatology, maternal-fetal medicine, critical care, anesthesia, neonatology. Stabilize: correct hypoglycemia (IV dextrose 10-50% as needed), correct coagulopathy (vitamin K 10mg IV, FFP if actively bleeding or INR >2 prior to delivery), IV fluids, N-acetylcysteine (NAC 150mg/kg IV load then maintenance — may benefit hepatic recovery). Evaluate for delivery: AFLP is an obstetric emergency; prompt delivery is the definitive treatment.

pathways.criteria: Confirmed AFLP at any gestational age after stabilization
pathways.treatment: DELIVER within 24-48h of diagnosis, often within hours if severe. If <34w: give betamethasone 12mg IM q24h x 2 doses only if delivery can be delayed 24-48h; do NOT delay if maternal instability. Mode: vaginal delivery preferred if cervix favorable and no fetal distress (induction of labor). Cesarean if unfavorable cervix, fetal distress, or maternal deterioration. Avoid general anesthesia (hepatotoxic agents, risk of worsening liver failure). Regional anesthesia if platelets >50,000 and no coagulopathy. Meticulous hemostasis. Prepare for postpartum hemorrhage.

pathways.criteria: After delivery of baby and placenta
pathways.treatment: Continue ICU monitoring for 48-72h (AFLP may worsen before improving). Correct hypoglycemia aggressively. Monitor LFTs, INR, glucose, renal function q6h. NAC IV 3-day protocol. Manage DIC with component therapy (platelets, FFP, cryoprecipitate). Renal replacement therapy if AKI persists. Most patients improve within 48-72h; if not improving, consider liver transplant evaluation. Counsel: AFLP recurs in 10-20% of subsequent pregnancies. Test mother and baby for LCHAD deficiency (genetic testing). Baby: monitor for hypoglycemia, LCHAD deficiency symptoms; dietary management (MCT oil).

pathways.criteria: Progressive deterioration despite delivery: worsening encephalopathy, INR >4, hypoglycemia refractory, multiorgan failure
pathways.treatment: Transplant center consultation. King's College Criteria for transplant in AFLP. Bridging: MARS (albumin dialysis), plasmapheresis, continuous RRT. Most AFLP patients recover with time and supportive care; transplant rarely needed (<5%). If transplant: maternal outcomes are good. Long-term recovery is typically complete in survivors.
